source_file: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

heuristics.rule: If you are Chinese or Indian background, seriously consider the Canada route before committing to US green card
heuristics.condition: When a Chinese or Indian international student or worker is deciding between US and Canada immigration paths
heuristics.action: Evaluate EB-2/EB-3 priority date backlog for your country of birth before assuming you can get a US green card within your working career
heuristics.because: EB-2 India priority dates were cited as being from 2012 as of the poster's graduation year, meaning decades-long waits; multiple commenters corroborated this with personal experience

heuristics.rule: Canadian Express Entry is rule-based, not lottery-based — plan accordingly
heuristics.condition: When comparing US H-1B lottery uncertainty to Canadian immigration pathways
heuristics.action: Treat CRS score optimization as a controllable variable unlike H-1B lottery; build a plan around hitting a target CRS score
heuristics.because: Poster explicitly contrasted H-1B lottery (random) with Express Entry ITA (score-based threshold), and this framing received high engagement

heuristics.rule: PGWP open work permit status removes job-change anxiety that H-1B status creates
heuristics.condition: When a worker values career mobility and the ability to switch employers without immigration risk
heuristics.action: Factor open work permit freedom into the Canada vs. US comparison, not just salary
heuristics.because: Poster described H-1B transfer 60-day window as high psychological pressure; PGWP's open nature was cited as a key structural advantage appearing across multiple comments

decision_factors.factor: Country of birth for US green card backlog
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Chinese and Indian nationals face decades-long EB-2/EB-3 waits due to per-country caps; nationals of most other countries face waits of a few years or less — this single variable can make US permanent residency practically unattainable within a career
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Look up current USCIS Visa Bulletin priority dates for your country of birth and employment-based category; calculate realistic wait time against your age and career horizon

decision_factors.factor: Salary gap between US and Canada (especially senior-level)
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A 30-50% salary gap at senior levels represents a significant lifetime earnings difference; this gap was acknowledged even by the pro-Canada poster
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Compare total compensation (base + equity + benefits) for equivalent roles in target cities (e.g., Bay Area vs. Toronto/Vancouver); note that commenters questioned whether the gap has narrowed recently

decision_factors.why_it_matters: H-1B lottery failures and green card wait anxiety impose real psychological burden that affects career decisions, job mobility, and quality of life — mentioned by multiple commenters as underweighted in typical analyses
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask honestly: how did you feel during H-1B lottery season? How would you feel being unable to leave a bad employer due to transfer risk?

decision_factors.factor: Industry and role availability in Canada
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Some industries (e.g., top-tier tech, certain finance roles) have significantly fewer opportunities in Canada, making a Canada-first strategy a real career trade-off for some fields
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research specific employer and role density in your field in Toronto/Vancouver vs. your US target market

decision_factors.factor: Housing cost in major Canadian cities
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Toronto and Vancouver housing prices offset some of Canada's lower cost-of-living advantage relative to US cities; smaller Canadian cities are more affordable but have fewer job opportunities
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Compare housing cost-to-income ratio in your target Canadian city vs. US city; consider whether remote work enables smaller-city living

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying on US H-1B path hoping to eventually get a green card
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: For Indian and Chinese nationals, the wait can exceed an entire working career — the green card may never arrive before retirement, meaning decades of visa-dependent employment anxiety
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Indian and Chinese nationals who entered the US employment system without fully researching per-country EB priority date backlogs

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing Canada for lower immigration stress
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Senior-level salary gap is real and compounds over time; engineers who stay in Canada forgo significant lifetime earnings compared to US counterparts, especially at senior and staff levels
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: High-earning tech workers, especially those in senior or staff+ roles where US equity compensation is largest

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Getting Canadian PR and then working in the US
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Canadian PR requires maintaining residency (730 days in 5 years); working in the US long-term can put PR status at risk — this was raised as a question with no clear resolution in the thread
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: People who hope to maintain Canadian PR as a backup while maximizing US career earnings

hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The 60-day grace period for job changes creates severe negotiating disadvantage — you cannot easily walk away from a bad offer or take time to find the right role without risking status
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: H-1B workers in toxic work environments or those who want to be selective in job searching

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming US green card is achievable within a reasonable timeline regardless of country of birth
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students research H-1B process carefully but often overlook or underweight the per-country green card backlog; the problem only becomes viscerally real years into the process
failure_patterns.consequence: Years or decades spent in H-1B limbo with no green card; career and life decisions constrained by employer dependency
warning_signs: Planning US career assuming green card in 5-10 years without checking priority date for your birth country
Not knowing your EB category's current priority date before starting a US job

failure_patterns.pattern: Underweighting psychological cost of immigration uncertainty in career planning
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Salary and career opportunity are quantifiable; psychological stress is not, so it gets systematically discounted in rational-seeming analyses
failure_patterns.consequence: Years of high anxiety around H-1B renewal, lottery, job changes, and layoffs; decision paralysis during critical career moments
warning_signs: Framing the US vs. Canada decision purely in terms of salary and career opportunity
Dismissing 'stress' as a soft factor not worth quantifying

failure_patterns.pattern: H-1B lottery failure leading to rushed or suboptimal decisions
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Two or more consecutive lottery failures create panic; workers feel they must take any available option rather than plan strategically
failure_patterns.consequence: Accepting bad job offers, leaving the US prematurely, or pivoting to Canada without proper preparation
warning_signs: Entering third H-1B lottery without a backup plan
No Canada work authorization or job pipeline developed during STEM OPT period

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your country of birth and which EB category would your US employer sponsor you for?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether a US green card is realistically attainable within your career, or whether you face a multi-decade wait
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If India or China EB-2/EB-3: look up current priority date and calculate realistic wait; if it exceeds 10-15 years, the Canada path warrants serious consideration
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If not India or China: US green card wait is likely manageable; weigh salary and career factors more heavily

diagnostic_questions.question: After getting Canadian PR, do you plan to work in the US? Have you checked the PR residency obligation?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person understands that Canadian PR has a physical presence requirement (730 days in 5 years) that can be violated by long-term US work
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If planning extended US work: consult an immigration lawyer about maintaining PR status; do not assume PR is a passive 'backup'
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If staying in Canada: residency obligation is straightforward to meet

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you already started H-1B transfer processing at the new employer before leaving the old one?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Under portability rules, a worker may be able to start at the new employer once transfer is filed (not approved) — but many workers don't know this and wait unnecessarily or miss the 60-day window
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If transfer is filed: confirm with an immigration attorney whether you can start work under portability
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If not filed: ensure you file before Day 60 of the grace period; do not wait for approval to start

industry_realities.common_belief: If you work hard and stay in the US, you'll eventually get your green card
industry_realities.actual_reality: For Indian and Chinese nationals in EB-2/EB-3, the backlog is so severe that many will not receive a green card before retirement age; this is a structural feature of the per-country cap system, not a temporary delay
industry_realities.implication: Indian and Chinese nationals should treat US permanent residency as unlikely within a normal career horizon and plan accordingly — either pursuing Canada or accepting indefinite H-1B status

industry_realities.common_belief: The salary gap between US and Canada has narrowed significantly in recent years
industry_realities.actual_reality: This was raised as a question by a commenter (rank=8) but not confirmed; the original poster cited 30-50% gaps for senior engineers, and no informed commenter provided data contradicting this for senior-level roles
industry_realities.implication: Do not assume the gap has closed without independently verifying current compensation data for your specific role and seniority level

industry_realities.common_belief: Express Entry is essentially a lottery like H-1B
industry_realities.actual_reality: Express Entry is a points-based system (CRS score) with transparent cutoffs; while cutoff scores fluctuate, the system rewards controllable factors (language scores, education, work experience, provincial nominations) unlike H-1B which is purely random
industry_realities.implication: Candidates can actively improve their CRS score and time their application strategically, unlike H-1B where the only lever is applying and hoping

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How the US vs. Canada decision is framed
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Compares only salary and job opportunities; treats immigration as a background administrative process that will eventually resolve itself
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats immigration pathway certainty and timeline as a primary variable alongside salary; explicitly calculates green card probability given country of birth before committing to either path
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Naive framing leads to decade-long commitments to a path that may never yield permanent residency; informed framing allows early course correction

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Views H-1B transfer as a minor administrative step; assumes job changes are nearly as free as for a green card holder
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Recognizes the 60-day grace period as a structural negotiating disadvantage; factors this into salary negotiation, employer selection, and emergency fund planning
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Workers who underestimate H-1B dependency accept worse conditions, stay in bad jobs longer, and are more vulnerable during layoffs

community_misconceptions.misconception: You can get Canadian PR and then work in the US long-term without any risk to your PR status
community_misconceptions.why_believed: PR sounds permanent, and people conflate 'permanent resident' with 'citizenship-level freedom to be abroad'; the residency obligation is not widely understood
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Canadian PR requires 730 days of physical presence in Canada within every 5-year rolling window; extended US work assignments can cause PR holders to fall out of compliance and lose their PR
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: PR can be lost or not renewed if residency obligation is not met; people who treat PR as a passive backup while maximizing US career time may find their Canadian status lapsed

community_misconceptions.misconception: On H-1B, you must wait for the transfer to be approved before starting work at the new employer
community_misconceptions.why_believed: People conflate H-1B 'approval' with 'authorization to work'; the transfer process feels like it requires a new approval before any work begins
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Under H-1B portability, a worker can generally begin working for the new employer once the H-1B transfer petition is filed (not approved), as long as the worker is maintaining valid status and the previous H-1B was approved
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Workers who wait for approval may unnecessarily miss job opportunities, lose income, or mismanage their 60-day grace period

real_case_outcomes.situation: Chinese or Indian background tech worker on F-1→OPT→STEM OPT→H-1B in the US, faced H-1B lottery twice without winning, finally won on third attempt, then confronted EB-2 green card backlog of 10+ years
real_case_outcomes.decision: Transferred to Canada, obtained PR through Express Entry within approximately 8 months of qualifying
real_case_outcomes.outcome: Achieved permanent residency in Canada; reported significantly lower psychological stress compared to US H-1B period; acknowledged real salary trade-off
real_case_outcomes.lesson: For Chinese and Indian nationals, the Canada Express Entry path can deliver permanent residency faster and more reliably than the US green card path, at the cost of a meaningful salary reduction — this trade-off is real and should be explicitly evaluated rather than assumed away

psychological_barriers.trigger: Repeatedly going through H-1B lottery and green card wait without a clear endpoint
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Chronic uncertainty and loss of agency; the immigration system is experienced as an unpredictable external force controlling major life decisions
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Workers stay in bad jobs, avoid negotiating salary, delay personal life decisions (marriage, children, buying property), and feel unable to plan beyond 1-2 years
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Reframe the question from 'will I eventually get my green card?' to 'what is the realistic probability and timeline given my birth country, and does that match my life goals?' — force an explicit calculation rather than indefinite hope

psychological_barriers.trigger: Comparing Canada salary to US salary and feeling the Canada option is 'settling'
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Loss aversion and status anxiety — the salary difference feels like a defeat rather than a trade-off with real compensating benefits
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Workers stay on the US path longer than is rational given their green card timeline, rationalizing continued H-1B extensions as 'just a little longer'
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Quantify the psychological cost of immigration uncertainty as if it were a salary deduction — ask 'how much would I pay per year to never worry about visa status again?' — then compare that number to the actual salary gap

reframing_moves.original_framing: Should I stay in the US or go to Canada? (implicitly: which country is better?)
reframing_moves.reframe: Given your country of birth, what is the realistic probability that you will receive a US green card within your working career — and does that probability justify the salary premium and immigration stress?
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Shifts from a country-comparison frame (subjective, emotionally loaded) to a probability and timeline calculation (concrete, personal, actionable); makes the green card backlog a central variable rather than an afterthought
reframing_moves.example_language: 如果你是印度/中国背景，认真考虑加拿大路线。不是说美国不好，是绿卡这件事在你的职业生涯内可能实现不了

_extraction_key: 1p3a_cleaned_canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度
